title: Interpreter and environment

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Interpreter and environment; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapters 2-4 and 8: interpreter, first steps, control flow, and errors; atomic topic: Interpreter and environment
sources.claim: A Python process has a concrete interpreter version, executable path, arguments, working directory, import path, and environment; all can change observable script behavior.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; sys, pathlib, traceback, importlib, and runtime-services documentation; atomic topic: Interpreter and environment
sources.claim: A Python process has a concrete interpreter version, executable path, arguments, working directory, import path, and environment; all can change observable script behavior.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Python process has a concrete interpreter version, executable path, arguments, working directory, import path, and environment; all can change observable script behavior.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The learner prints sys.executable, sys.version, sys.argv, and Path.cwd() to explain why an editor run finds a dependency that a shell run does not. Your goal is to diagnose a script that runs differently because of interpreter or working-directory selection. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Scripts, indentation and main guard; pf tools files. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Python process has a concrete interpreter version, executable path, arguments, working directory, import path, and environment; all can change observable script behavior. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Activating a virtual environment mainly changes command lookup, relative paths resolve from the process working directory, PYTHONPATH can shadow modules, and editor-selected interpreters may differ from the terminal. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A .py file always runs with the interpreter beside it and relative files resolve from the script's own directory.

includes: Identify interpreter version, arguments, working directory, module path and environment effects.
Diagnose a script that runs differently because of interpreter or working-directory selection.
excludes: Activating a virtual environment mainly changes command lookup, relative paths resolve from the process working directory, PYTHONPATH can shadow modules, and editor-selected interpreters may differ from the terminal.

outcomes: Diagnose a script that runs differently because of interpreter or working-directory selection.

walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: The learner prints sys.executable, sys.version, sys.argv, and Path.cwd() to explain why an editor run finds a dependency that a shell run does not.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Diagnose the actual process context: verify sys.executable, version, arguments, working directory, and import path before changing code or dependencies.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Diagnose the actual process context: verify sys.executable, version, arguments, working directory, and import path before changing code or dependencies.

examples.title: Interpreter and environment: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A local file named json.py shadows the standard-library module, so inspecting module.__file__ reveals the search-path collision.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A local file named json.py shadows the standard-library module, so inspecting module.__file__ reveals the search-path collision.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Activating a virtual environment mainly changes command lookup, relative paths resolve from the process working directory, PYTHONPATH can shadow modules, and editor-selected interpreters may differ from the terminal.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A .py file always runs with the interpreter beside it and relative files resolve from the script's own directory.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Diagnose a script that runs differently because of interpreter or working-directory selection.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A .py file always runs with the interpreter beside it and relative files resolve from the script's own directory.
misconceptions.correction: Diagnose the actual process context: verify sys.executable, version, arguments, working directory, and import path before changing code or dependencies.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A .py file always runs with the interpreter beside it and relative files resolve from the script's own directory.

analogy.example: an experiment notebook that becomes a saved procedure executed by one identified interpreter in a known workspace can introduce Interpreter and environment, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: REPL experiments, scripts, environments, and tracebacks
limitations: import caching, module paths, display hooks, indentation grammar, and traceback chaining are Python-specific

recap.summary: You should now be able to diagnose a script that runs differently because of interpreter or working-directory selection. Keep this boundary in view: Activating a virtual environment mainly changes command lookup, relative paths resolve from the process working directory, PYTHONPATH can shadow modules, and editor-selected interpreters may differ from the terminal.

Predict this case from memory: A local file named json.py shadows the standard-library module, so inspecting module.__file__ reveals the search-path collision.
